chapters.text:
There are two ways, one of life and one of death, and there is a great difference between the two ways. Now this is the way of life: first, you shall love God who made you; second, your neighbor as yourself. And whatever you would not want to happen to you, do not do to another.

And the teaching of these words is this: bless those who curse you, and pray for your enemies, and fast for those who persecute you. For what credit is it if you love those who love you? Do not even the Gentiles do the same? But you, love those who hate you, and you will not have an enemy.

Abstain from fleshly and worldly desires. If anyone gives you a blow on the right cheek, turn to him the other also, and you will be perfect. If anyone compels you for one mile, go with him two. If anyone takes away your cloak, give him your tunic also. If anyone takes from you what is yours, do not demand it back, for you are not able. Give to everyone who asks you, and do not demand it back; for the Father's will is that giving be done to all from his own gracious gifts. Blessed is the one who gives according to the commandment, for he is innocent. But woe to the one who receives. For if someone receives because he has need, he will be innocent; but the one who has no need will pay the penalty: why he received, and for what purpose. And having come under confinement, he will be examined concerning the things he did, and he will not come out from there until he repays the last quadrans.

But about this, it has also been said: Let your alms sweat in your hands until you know to whom you should give.

footnotes.text: 'Gentiles' could also be rendered 'nations'; here it refers to those outside the covenant community.
A quadrans was a very small Roman coin.
The saying 'Let your alms sweat in your hands' is a vivid metaphor urging discernment in giving rather than impulsive distribution.

chapters.text: And the second commandment of the teaching is this: You shall not murder; you shall not commit adultery; you shall not corrupt boys;a you shall not commit sexual immorality; you shall not steal; you shall not practice magic; you shall not use sorcery; you shall not murder a child by abortion, nor kill one that has been born.b You shall not desire the things of your neighbor; you shall not swear falsely; you shall not bear false witness; you shall not speak evil; you shall not hold a grudge. You shall not be double-minded nor double-tongued, for the double tongue is a snare of death. Your word shall not be false or empty, but filled with action.c You shall not be greedy, nor grasping, nor a hypocrite, nor ill-disposed, nor arrogant. You shall not form an evil plan against your neighbor. You shall not hate any man;d but some you shall reprove, for some you shall pray, and some you shall love more than your own life.

footnotes.text: Or 'abuse boys' or 'commit pederasty'; the Greek term refers specifically to the sexual corruption of boys.
Literally, 'you shall not murder a child in destruction, nor kill one that has been born'—a twofold prohibition covering destruction before birth and killing after birth.
Or 'fulfilled by action.' The Greek literally means 'having been filled with action/deed.'
Greek 'any man' uses a masculine singular form with representative force.

chapters.text: My child, flee from every evil thing, and from everything like it. Do not become quick-tempered, for anger leads to murder; nor jealous, nor quarrelsome, nor hot-headed, for from all these murders are born. My child, do not become lustful, for lust leads to sexual immorality;a nor foul-mouthed, nor haughty-eyed, for from all these adulteries are born. My child, do not become an observer of omens, since it leads into idolatry; nor an enchanter, nor an astrologer, nor one who performs purifications, nor even be willing to look upon them; for from all these idolatry is born. My child, do not become a liar, since lying leads to theft; nor money-loving, nor vainglorious, for from all these thefts are born. My child, do not become a grumbler, since it leads to blasphemy;b nor self-willed, nor evil-minded, for from all these blasphemies are born. But be meek, since the meek will inherit the earth.c Become patient and merciful and guileless and quiet and good, and always trembling at the words that you have heard. You shall not exalt yourself, nor give bold arrogance to your soul. Your soul shall not be joined with the lofty, but you shall associate with the righteous and humble. You shall accept the events that befall you as good,d knowing that apart from God nothing happens.

footnotes.text: The Greek source text has πορείαν ('journey'), but the context of lust leading to adultery strongly suggests this is a scribal error for the common moral term πορνείαν, 'sexual immorality' or 'fornication'.
'Blasphemy' here may also carry the broader sense of slander or abusive speech, though the religious force of speech against God suits the context well.
A direct quotation from Matthew 5:5, which itself draws on Psalm 37:11.

The Greek phrase, `τὰ συμβαίνοντά σοι ἐνεργήματα`, is difficult. `ἐνεργήματα` means 'workings' or 'effects,' suggesting that events are not random but are active operations. The translation 'the events that befall you' captures the main idea of providential circumstances in a more accessible way.

chapters.text: My child, remember night and day the one who speaks to you the word of God, and you shall honor him as the Lord; for where lordship is spoken, there the Lord is. And you shall seek out day by day the faces of the saints, so that you may find rest in their words. You shall not make a schism, but you shall reconcile those who are fighting. You shall judge justly; you shall not show partiality when reproving transgressions. You shall not be double-minded, whether it will be or not. Do not be one who stretches out his hands to receive, but to give, draws them in. If you have anything, through your hands you shall give a ransom for your sins. You shall not hesitate to give, nor grumble while giving; for you will know who is the good repayer of the reward. You shall not turn away from the one in need, but you shall share all things with your brother, and you shall not say they are your own; for if you are sharers in what is immortal, how much more in mortal things? You shall not take your hand away from your son or from your daughter, but from youth you shall teach them the fear of God. You shall not command your slave or your female slave in your bitterness—they who hope in the same God—lest they should fail to fear the God who is over both; for he does not come to call according to outward standing, but upon those whom the Spirit has prepared. But you slaves shall be subject to your masters as to a pattern of God, in reverence and fear. You shall hate all hypocrisy and everything that is not pleasing to the Lord. You shall never forsake the commandments of the Lord, but you shall keep what you received, neither adding nor taking away. In the assembly you shall confess your transgressions, and you shall not come to your prayer with an evil conscience. This is the way of life.

footnotes.text: Or 'the presence/persons of the saints.' The Greek literally says 'the faces of the saints,' an idiom for seeking their company.
'Schism' preserves the ecclesial sense of a divisive rupture within the community.
The phrase 'a ransom for your sins' reflects early Christian language linking almsgiving with repentance and mercy; the wording is intentionally left strong because the Greek is strong.
Or 'according to appearance/face'; the point is that God does not act based on social status.

The Greek αἰσχύνη can mean 'shame,' but in this context of submission alongside 'fear' (φόβος), it carries the positive sense of 'reverence' or 'respect,' avoiding the modern English connotation of disgrace.
'In the assembly' may refer to the gathered church community rather than a building.

chapters.text: But the way of death is this: first of all, it is evil and full of curse: murders, adulteries, lusts, sexual immoralities, thefts, idolatries, sorceries, magic arts, robberies, false testimonies, hypocrisies, double-heartedness, deceit, arrogance, malice, self-will, greed, foul speech, jealousy, audacity, haughtiness, boastfulness; persecutors of the good, hating truth, loving falsehood, not knowing the reward of righteousness, not cleaving to what is good nor to righteous judgment, keeping watch not for what is good but for what is evil; from whom gentleness and patience are far, loving vain things, pursuing recompense, not showing mercy to a poor man, not laboring on behalf of one who is oppressed, not knowing the one who made them, murderers of children, destroyers of God's handiwork, turning away from the one in need, oppressing the one under affliction, advocates of the rich, lawless judges of the poor, altogether sinful. Be delivered, children, from all these things.

footnotes.text: Or 'full of cursing.' The Greek permits either the state of being accursed or being characterized by curses.

The paired terms rendered 'sorceries' (μαγεῖαι) and 'magic arts' (φαρμακεῖαι) overlap in meaning; the translation distinguishes them to preserve the Greek doublet. The latter term is often associated with the use of drugs or potions.
'Murderers of children' likely includes the common ancient practice of infant exposure, as well as abortion.
Or 'May you be delivered.' The Greek optative expresses a strong wish or prayer for deliverance.

chapters.text: See that no one leads you astray from this way of the teaching, since he teaches you apart from God. For if you are able to bear the whole yoke of the Lord, you will be perfect; but if you are not able, do what you can. And concerning food, bear what you are able; but be strictly on guard against what is sacrificed to idols, for it is the worship of dead gods.

footnotes.text: Or 'Take care that no one deceives you from this way of the teaching.'
'Perfect' may also be understood as 'complete' or 'mature.'
Greek λίαν πρόσεχε. Or 'be exceedingly careful about', or 'strictly keep away from'.
That is, food offered in pagan sacrifice.
Or 'lifeless gods.'

chapters.text: Now concerning baptism, baptize in this way: Having first proclaimed all these things, baptize into the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit in living water. But if you do not have living water, baptize into other water; and if you cannot in cold, then in warm. But if you have neither, pour water on the head three times into the name of Father and Son and Holy Spirit. But before the baptism, let the one baptizing and the one being baptized fast beforehand, and any others who are able; and you shall command the one being baptized to fast beforehand for one or two days.

footnotes.text: The Greek verbs for 'baptize' in this sentence are plural, addressing the community. From verse 2 ('if you do not have...'), the verbs shift to the singular, addressing the individual performing the baptism.
“Into the name” follows the Greek preposition εἰς (eis) more closely than the traditional English “in the name,” emphasizing entrance into a relationship.
“Living water” refers to flowing water, such as from a spring or river, as opposed to stagnant water in a cistern or pool. The literal phrase is kept for its scriptural resonance (cf. John 4:10).
The Greek is elliptical, literally 'beforehand for one or two.' Days are implied by the context of fasting.

chapters.text:
But let your fasts not be with the hypocrites, for they fast on the second day of the week and on the fifth; but you, fast on the fourth day and on Preparation. Neither pray as the hypocrites, but as the Lord commanded in his gospel, pray in this way:

Our Father in heaven,
may your name be made holy,
may your kingdom come,
may your will be done,
as in heaven, so also on earth.
Give us today our bread for the day,
and forgive us our debt,
as we also forgive our debtors,
and do not bring us into testing,
but rescue us from the evil one;
for yours is the power and the glory unto the ages.

Pray in this way three times a day.

footnotes.text: 'Preparation' was the customary name for the day before the Sabbath, that is, Friday.
The Greek here has 'in heaven' (singular), a slight variation from the 'in the heavens' (plural) common in Matthew's version of the prayer.
Or 'hallowed be your name.'
The Greek adjective behind 'for the day' is rare and debated; alternatives include 'daily,' 'necessary,' or 'for tomorrow.'
This version uses the singular 'debt,' whereas Matthew's version often has the plural 'debts' or 'trespasses.'
'Testing' may also be rendered 'temptation' or 'trial.'
Or 'from evil.'

The prayer closely parallels Matthew 6:9–13, but this version includes a shorter doxology than is found in many later manuscripts of Matthew. The Didache presents it as the Lord's command 'in his gospel' within its church-order instruction.

chapters.text:
Now concerning the thanksgiving, give thanks in this way. First, concerning the cup:

We give thanks to you, our Father,
for the holy vine of David your servant,
which you made known to us through Jesus your servant.
To you be the glory forever.

And concerning the broken bread:

We give thanks to you, our Father,
for the life and knowledge
which you made known to us through Jesus your servant.
To you be the glory forever.

Just as this broken piece was scattered upon the mountains and, when gathered together, became one, so let your assembly be gathered together from the ends of the earth into your kingdom. For yours is the glory and the power through Jesus the Messiah forever.

But let no one eat or drink from your thanksgiving except those who have been baptized into the name of the Lord. For also concerning this, the Lord has said: “Do not give what is holy to the dogs.”

footnotes.text: Or 'Eucharist'; the Greek term means 'thanksgiving' and likely refers here to the communal sacred meal and its prayers.
'Your servant' could also be rendered 'your child.' The Greek term does not explicitly say 'son' in this line.
Matthew 7:6 is the likely source of the saying, 'Do not give what is holy to the dogs.'

chapters.text:
But after being filled, give thanks in this way:

We give thanks to you, Holy Father,
for your holy name,
which you caused to dwell in our hearts,
and for the knowledge and faith and immortality
which you made known to us through Jesus your servant;
to you be the glory forever.

You, Master Almighty,
created all things for the sake of your name,
and gave food and drink to men for enjoyment,
so that they might give thanks to you;
but to us you graciously gave spiritual food and drink
and eternal life through your servant.
Above all we give thanks to you because you are mighty;
to you be the glory forever.

Remember, Lord, your church:
to deliver it from every evil
and to perfect it in your love;
and gather it, the sanctified one,
from the four winds into your kingdom,
which you prepared for it.
For yours is the power and the glory forever.

Let grace come,
and let this world pass away.
Hosanna to the son of David.
If anyone is holy, let him come;
if anyone is not, let him repent.
Maranatha. Amen.

But permit the prophets to give thanks as much as they desire.

footnotes.text: Or 'child' for 'your servant' (παῖς); the Greek term can bear either sense here.
'From the four winds' is biblical imagery for gathering the scattered people from every direction.
Maranatha is an Aramaic liturgical cry, likely meaning 'Our Lord, come.'

The manuscript reads 'to the God of David' (τῷ θεῷ Δαβίδ). Most editors emend the text to 'to the son of David' (τῷ υἱῷ Δαβίδ) to align with the messianic acclamation in the Gospels (e.g., Matt. 21:9).

chapters.text:
Therefore, whoever comes and teaches you all these things that have been said before, receive him. But if the teacher himself turns aside and teaches another teaching so as to overthrow these things, do not listen to him. But if it is to add to righteousness and knowledge of the Lord, receive him as the Lord.

Now concerning the apostles and prophets, according to the ordinance of the gospel, act in this way. Let every apostle who comes to you be received as the Lord. He shall remain for only one day; but if there is need, also the next. But if he remains three days, he is a false prophet. And when the apostle departs, let him take nothing except bread, until he finds lodging; but if he asks for money, he is a false prophet.

And every prophet speaking in the Spirit you shall not test, nor shall you judge; for every sin will be forgiven, but this sin will not be forgiven. Yet not everyone speaking in the Spirit is a prophet, but only if he has the ways of the Lord. So then, from their ways the false prophet and the prophet will be known. And every prophet who appoints a table in the Spirit shall not eat from it; otherwise he is a false prophet. And every prophet teaching the truth, if he does not do what he teaches, is a false prophet. But every prophet who has been tested and is genuine, acting for the earthly mystery of the church, yet not teaching others to do all that he himself does, shall not be judged by you; for he has his judgment with God. For in the same way the ancient prophets also acted.

But whoever says in the Spirit, “Give me money,” or something else, you shall not listen to him. Yet if he tells you to give for others who are in need, let no one judge him.

footnotes.text: The Greek text for the apostle's length of stay is likely corrupt. It reads 'he shall not remain one day.' This translation follows the common emendation that he should remain for 'only one day.'
Or, 'until he lodges' or 'until he reaches his night's shelter.' The Greek concerns obtaining lodging for the night on a journey.
The warning about a sin that 'will not be forgiven' echoes the dominical saying about blasphemy against the Holy Spirit; compare Matt 12:31; Mark 3:28–29; Luke 12:10.

Discernment is not abolished. The prohibition in the previous sentence concerns irreverent testing of inspired speech, not the suspension of moral evaluation, which this verse immediately requires. Prophets are to be known 'from their ways.'
'Appoints a table' may refer to directing or setting out a meal while speaking in the Spirit, possibly in a liturgical or symbolic setting.

The clause 'acting for the earthly mystery of the church' is obscure. 'Earthly' (κοσμικός) here likely means 'in the world' as opposed to 'heavenly,' not 'sinful.' The phrase may refer to a symbolic action performed for the church's life in the world. The translation keeps the ambiguity rather than forcing a single interpretation.

chapters.text: But let everyone who comes in the name of the Lord be received; then, after testing him, you will know him, for you will have understanding of right and left. But if the one who comes is a traveler, help him as much as you can; he shall not remain with you for more than two or three days, should there be a need. But if he wants to settle among you and is a craftsman, let him work and eat. But if he has no trade, provide, according to your understanding, how a Christian may not live idle among you. But if he is not willing to do so, he is a Christ-trader; watch out for such people.

footnotes.text: This phrase, 'understanding of right and left,' is an idiom for discernment.
Or 'craft,' 'skill.'
The rare Greek term χριστέμπορος (christemporos) means one who makes a profit from Christ, treating the faith as a commodity.

chapters.text: But every true prophet who wishes to settle among you is worthy of his food. Likewise a true teacher is himself also worthy, like the worker, of his food. Therefore, taking every firstfruit of the produce of winepress and threshing floor, and of cattle and sheep, you shall give it to the prophets, for they are your high priests. But if you do not have a prophet, give it to the poor. If you make bread,a take the firstfruit and give it according to the commandment. Likewise, when you open a jar of wine or oil, take the firstfruit and give it to the prophets. And of silver and clothing and every possession, take the firstfruit,b as it seems good to you, and give it according to the commandment.

footnotes.text: The normalized source text has the difficult reading Ἐὰν σιτιαυτοῖς. The manuscript reading is likely Ἐὰν σιτίαν ποιῇς, 'If you make a batch of bread.' The translation follows this sense, which fits the context of giving firstfruits from prepared food.
'Firstfruit' here refers to the first portion of produce or goods set aside and given, echoing biblical firstfruits practice.

chapters.text: But on the Lord’s own day, after you have gathered together, break bread and give thanks, after first confessing your transgressions, so that your sacrifice may be pure. But let no one who has a dispute with his companion come together with you until they are reconciled, so that your sacrifice may not be defiled. For this is what was spoken by the Lord: “In every place and time offer me a pure sacrifice, because I am a great King,” says the Lord, “and my name is marvelous among the nations.”

footnotes.text: The opening phrase may also be rendered 'On the Lord’s Day.' The Greek expression is emphatic and somewhat unusual.
The citation is a conflation and adaptation of Malachi 1:11 and 1:14 (LXX).
'Sacrifice' is retained because the Greek uses explicit sacrificial language (*thusia*). In this context, it refers to the community's entire act of worship, centered on the Eucharistic thanksgiving.

chapters.text: Appoint, then, for yourselves overseers and deacons worthy of the Lord—men gentle and not lovers of money, and truthful and proven; for they too minister to you the ministry of the prophets and teachers. Therefore do not disregard them, for they are your honored ones together with the prophets and teachers. And reprove one another, not in anger but in peace, as you have it in the gospel; and let no one speak to anyone who goes wrong against another, nor let him hear from you, until he repents. But perform your prayers and your acts of mercy and all your deeds in this way, as you have it in the gospel of our Lord.

footnotes.text: Or 'Elect' or 'Ordain' for 'Appoint'; the Greek can include the sense of selecting leaders, possibly with election in view.
'Overseers' translates the Greek term directly; 'bishops' reflects the later church title derived from the same word.
'Acts of mercy' may also be rendered 'alms' or 'almsgiving.'
The phrase 'as you have it in the gospel' likely refers to the community's received gospel teaching as an authoritative norm for conduct.

chapters.text: Keep watch over your life.a Do not let your lamps be quenched, and do not let your loins be loosened,b but be ready, for you do not know the hour in which our Lord is coming. And gather together often, seeking the things that are fitting for your souls;c for the whole time of your faith will not profit you unless you are made complete in the last season. For in the last days false prophets and corrupters will be multiplied, and the sheep will be turned into wolves, and love will be turned into hatred. For as lawlessness increases, they will hate one another and persecute and betray, and then the world-deceiverd will appear as a son of Gode and will do signs and wonders, and the earth will be delivered into his hands, and he will do unlawful things such as have never happened since the age began. Then the creation of menf will come into the fire of testing, and many will be caused to stumble and will perish, but those who endure in their faith will be saved by the accursed one himself.g And then the signs of the truth will appear: first, a sign of an opening out in heaven;h then a sign of a trumpet's voice; and the third, the resurrection of the dead—yet not of all, but as it was said: The Lord will come, and all the holy ones with him.i Then the world will see the Lord coming upon the clouds of heaven.j

footnotes.text: Or 'for your life.' The Greek preposition ὑπέρ can carry a protective sense.
Literally 'do not let your loins be unloosed,' an image of readiness for action also found in biblical exhortation (e.g., Luke 12:35, Eph. 6:14).
Or 'what pertains to your souls' / 'what is beneficial for your souls.'
Greek ὁ κοσμοπλάνος, a rare apocalyptic term for the final Antichrist figure.
The Greek says ὡς υἱὸς Θεοῦ, 'as a son of God,' without the definite article, suggesting a claim to divine status rather than the specific title 'the Son of God.'
The phrase ἡ κτίσις τῶν ἀνθρώπων, 'the creation of men,' is unusual and may be understood more broadly as 'the human race' or 'mankind.'

The clause 'will be saved by the accursed one himself' (σωθήσονται ὑπ᾽ αὐτοῦ τοῦ καταθέματος) is textually and theologically difficult. Interpretations vary widely, and the transmitted Greek may be corrupt. Some suggest it means 'saved from under the curse itself' (i.e., the world-deceiver's reign).
The expression σημεῖον ἐκπετάσεως, rendered 'a sign of an opening out in heaven,' is rare and uncertain; alternatives include 'an outspreading' or 'an unfolding' in heaven.
A quotation echoing Zechariah 14:5.
The final line echoes the imagery of the Son of Man's coming in Daniel 7:13 and the Gospel tradition (e.g., Matt. 24:30, 26:64).

chapters.text:
Because of the sudden and repeated misfortunes and crises that have happened to us, brothers, we consider that we have been somewhat slow in turning our attention to the matters being sought from us among you, beloved—namely, the alien and strange uprising, defiled and unholy, foreign to the chosen of God, which a few rash and self-willed persons have kindled to such a pitch of madness that your dignified and widely spoken-of name, worthy of love among all people, has been greatly blasphemed.

For who, after staying among you, did not approve your all-virtuous and steadfast faith? Who did not admire your sober and gentle godliness in Messiah? Who did not proclaim the magnificent character of your hospitality? And who did not count blessed your complete and sure knowledge?

For you did everything without partiality, and you walked in the lawful ordinances of God, submitting yourselves to your leaders and giving the honor that is fitting to the elders among you. You were directing the younger men to think thoughts marked by moderation and dignity. And you were instructing the women to carry out all things with a blameless and dignified and pure conscience, loving their own husbands as is fitting; and, being within the rule of submission, to manage the affairs of the household with dignity, showing complete self-control.

footnotes.text: Or 'dissension,' 'rebellion,' or 'sedition' for στάσις; the term denotes a grave communal rupture, not a minor disagreement.
'In Messiah' translates ἐν Χριστῷ with Χριστός understood as a title; many English versions retain 'in Christ.'
'Brothers' renders ἀδελφοί. The address may include the whole congregation, but the Greek form is masculine.

chapters.text: And all of you were humble-minded, boasting in nothing, submitting rather than demanding submission, taking more pleasure in giving than in receiving; content with the provisions of the Messiah, and paying close attention to his words, you had embraced them carefully in your inward parts, and his sufferings were before your eyes. Thus deep and rich peace was given to all, and an insatiable longing for doing good, and a full outpouring of the Holy Spirit came upon all. And being full of holy purpose, in good eagerness and with devout confidence you stretched out your hands to God the Almighty, begging him to become merciful, if in anything you had sinned unwillingly. Day and night your concern was for the whole brotherhood, so that the number of his chosen ones might be saved with mercy and a good conscience. You were sincere and guileless and free from resentment toward one another. Every sedition and every schism was detestable to you. You mourned over the trespasses of your neighbors; you judged their shortcomings to be your own. Beneficent, ready for every good work, adorned with a venerable way of life, you carried everything through in his fear; the commandments and the righteous requirements of the Lord had been written on the broad places of your hearts.

footnotes.text: Or 'had taken them to heart.' The Greek speaks more bodily and vividly of embracing the Lord's words in the inward parts.
'Brotherhood' renders a masculine collective term for the community of believers.
'Schism' is an ecclesial term here, stronger than a mere disagreement or ordinary division.
The phrase 'on the broad places of your hearts' is a vivid Greek expression, likely echoing scriptural language about God's commands being inscribed inwardly.

chapters.text:
All glory and abundance were given to you, and what is written was fulfilled: “He ate and drank, and became broad and fat, and the beloved one kicked back.”

2 From this came jealousy and envy, strife and faction, persecution and disorder, war and captivity. 3 So the dishonored rose up against the honored, the inglorious against the glorious, the foolish against the prudent, the young against the elders. 4 Because of this, righteousness and peace have withdrawn far away, since each one abandoned the fear of God and became dim-sighted in his faith in him, neither walking in the lawful ways of his commandments nor conducting himself in a manner fitting for the Messiah, but each going according to the desires of his evil heart, having taken up unjust and ungodly jealousy, through which death also entered into the world.

footnotes.text: The quotation echoes Deuteronomy 32:15, adapted in wording. The image is of prosperity leading to arrogant resistance.
Or “became short-sighted in his faithfulness to him.” The Greek πίστις can denote both faith and faithfulness.
“Messiah” translates Χριστός as a title here; traditional English often has “Christ.”
The line about death entering the world likely evokes biblical patterns linking envy and violence, especially Cain.

chapters.text: For it is written thus: “And it came to pass after some days that Cain brought from the fruits of the earth an offering to God, and Abel also brought from the firstborn of the sheep and from their fat portions. And God looked upon Abel and upon his gifts, but upon Cain and upon his sacrifices he did not pay attention. And Cain was greatly grieved, and his face fell. And God said to Cain, ‘Why have you become deeply grieved, and why has your face fallen? If you offer rightly, but do not divide rightly, have you not sinned? Be still; his turning is toward you, and you shall rule over him.’ And Cain said to Abel his brother, ‘Let us go through into the field.’ And it came to pass, while they were in the field, Cain rose up against Abel his brother and killed him.” You see, brothers, jealousy and envy brought about brother-murder. Because of jealousy our father Jacob fled from the face of Esau his brother. Jealousy caused Joseph to be persecuted even to death and to come even into slavery. Jealousy compelled Moses to flee from the face of Pharaoh king of Egypt, when he heard from his fellow tribesman, ‘Who appointed you ruler or judge over us? Do you want to kill me, as you killed the Egyptian yesterday?’ Because of jealousy Aaron and Miriam lodged outside the camp. Jealousy brought Dathan and Abiram down alive into Hades because they rebelled against Moses, the attendant of God. Because of jealousy David incurred envy not only from the foreigners, but was also persecuted by Saul king of Israel.

footnotes.text: The wording of the Genesis citation is difficult in Greek and likely reflects the Septuagint form of Genesis 4:7. 'Do not divide rightly' may also be understood as 'do not apportion rightly' or 'do not distinguish rightly.'
The pair 'jealousy and envy' renders two related but distinct Greek terms, ζῆλος and φθόνος.
'Hades' is the Greek term for the realm of the dead.
See Numbers 16 for the rebellion of Dathan and Abiram against Moses.

chapters.text: But to stop with the examples of the ancients, let us come to the athletes who lived nearest to our own time. Let us take the noble examples from our own generation. Because of jealousy and envy, the greatest and most righteous pillars were persecuted and contended even to death. Let us set before our eyes the good apostles: Petros, who because of unrighteous jealousy endured not one or two but many sufferings, and so, having borne witness, went to the place of glory that was due him. Because of jealousy and strife, Paulos showed what is the prize of endurance: having worn chains seven times, having been driven into exile, having been stoned, having become a herald both in the east and in the west, he received the noble renown of his faith, having taught righteousness to the whole world and having come to the farthest limit of the west; and having borne witness before the rulers, so he was released from the world and went to the holy place, having become the greatest pattern of endurance.

footnotes.text: Or 'champions'; the Greek keeps an athletic-contest image throughout the chapter.
'Having borne witness' may also carry the sense 'having suffered martyrdom' in this context.

'The farthest limit of the west' is often understood as a reference to the western extremity of the Roman world; some identify it specifically with Spain, though the text itself does not name the place.
'Righteousness' could also be rendered 'justice' here.

chapters.text:
To these men, who had conducted themselves in holiness, there was gathered a great multitude of the elect, who, having suffered through jealousy many outrages and tortures, became a most beautiful example among us.

2. Because of jealousy, women were persecuted as Danaids and Dirces; after suffering terrible and unholy abuses, they reached the sure course of faith and received a noble reward, though weak in body.

3. Jealousy estranged wives from their husbands and altered what was spoken by our father Adam: “This now is bone from my bones and flesh from my flesh.”

4. Jealousy and strife have torn down great cities and uprooted great nations.

footnotes.text: Danaids and Dirce are figures from Greek myth. Clement invokes them allusively to describe the women’s persecution; the exact force of the comparison is debated.
Or “the firm race of faith.” The Greek δρόμος can evoke a race-course as well as a course or path.
Quotation from Gen 2:23.

chapters.text: These things, beloved, we have written not only to admonish you, but also to remind ourselves; for we are in the same arena, and the same struggle lies before us. Therefore let us leave behind empty and futile concerns, and let us come to the glorious and solemn rule of our tradition. And let us see what is good, and what is pleasing, and what is acceptable to the one who made us. Let us fix our gaze on the blood of the Messiah and understand how precious it is to God, his Father, because, having been poured out for our salvation, it brought the grace of repentance to the whole world. Let us pass through all the generations and learn carefully that in generation after generation the Master gave a place for repentance to those willing to turn back to him. Noah proclaimed repentance, and those who obeyed were saved. Yonah proclaimed destruction to the Ninevites; but when they repented of their sins, they made atonement before God by their supplication and received salvation, though they were strangers to God.

footnotes.text: Or 'the blood of Christ.' The Greek title Χριστός is rendered 'Messiah' here as a title.
'Brought the grace of repentance to the whole world' may also be understood as 'brought an opportunity for repentance to the whole world.'
Literally, 'gave a place for repentance.'
Or 'appeased God' / 'propitiated God'; the Greek verb can carry the sense of making atonement or seeking divine favor.
See Jonah 3 for the Ninevites' repentance after Jonah's proclamation of overthrow.

chapters.text: The ministers of the grace of God spoke through the Holy Spirit concerning repentance, and the Master himself of all things also spoke concerning repentance with an oath: “For as I live,” says the Lord, “I do not desire the death of the sinner so much as his repentance,” adding also a good intention: “Repent, O house of Israel, of your lawlessness. Say to the sons of my people, ‘Even if your sins should reach from the earth to heaven, and even if they are redder than scarlet and blacker than sackcloth, yet if you turn to me with your whole heart and say, “Father,” I will listen to you as to a holy people.’” And in another place he says thus: “Wash and become clean; remove your wicked deeds from before my eyes, cease from your wickednesses, learn to do good, seek justice, rescue the one being wronged, judge for the orphan, and give justice to the widow. And come, and let us reason together,” says the Lord. “Even if your sins are like crimson, I will whiten them like snow; and if they are like scarlet, I will whiten them like wool. But if you are willing and listen to me, you shall eat the good things of the land; but if you are not willing and do not listen to me, a sword will devour you; for the mouth of the Lord has spoken these things.” Therefore, since he desires all his beloved ones to share in repentance, he established it by his all-sovereign purpose.

footnotes.text: The wording echoes Ezekiel 33:11, but Clement's form includes an added benevolent clause not identical to standard Septuagint wording.
The citation is a composite paraenetic use of prophetic language rather than a verbatim quotation from a single extant Old Testament verse.
This section draws chiefly on Isaiah 1:16–20 in Septuagintal form.
Or 'he confirmed it by his almighty counsel.' The Greek can emphasize either God's sovereign purpose or the firmness with which he established this call to repentance.

chapters.text:
Therefore let us obey his majestic and glorious purpose, and, becoming suppliants of his mercy and kindness, let us fall before him and turn to his compassions, leaving behind fruitless toil, as well as strife and the jealousy that leads to death.

Let us fix our gaze on those who ministered perfectly to his majestic glory.

Let us take Enoch, who, having been found righteous in obedience, was transferred, and his death was not found.

Noah, having been found faithful, through his ministry proclaimed regeneration to the world, and through him the Master saved the living creatures that entered the ark in harmony, two by two.

footnotes.text: The wording 'his death was not found' closely follows the Greek's unusual expression and echoes scriptural language about Enoch rather than paraphrasing the sense.
Or 'renewal' or 'new beginning' for 'regeneration.' The Greek term is παλιγγενεσία, a weighty word for rebirth or renewal.
'The Master' renders δεσπότης, a term of absolute authority distinct from κύριος ('Lord').
The final phrase refers to the animals entering the ark as corresponding pairs; compare the flood narrative in Genesis 6–7.

chapters.text: Abraham, who was called the friend, was found faithful in that he became obedient to the words of God. 2 And in obedience he went out from his land and from his kindred and from his father’s house, so that, leaving behind a small land and a weak kindred and a little house, he might inherit the promises of God. For he says to him, 3 “Go out from your land and from your kindred and from your father’s house into the land that I will show you; and I will make you into a great nation, and I will bless you and magnify your name, and you will be blessed. And I will bless those who bless you, and I will curse those who curse you, and in you all the tribes of the earth will be blessed.” 4 And again, when he was separated from Lot, God said to him, “Lift up your eyes and see from the place where you now are, toward the north and the south and the east and the sea, because all the land that you see, I will give to you and to your seed forever. 5 And I will make your seed like the sand of the earth; if anyone is able to count the sand of the earth, your seed also will be counted.” 6 And again he says, “God brought Abraham out and said to him, ‘Look up to heaven and count the stars, if you are able to count them; so will your seed be.’” And Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him for righteousness. 7 Because of faith and hospitality a son was given to him in old age, and through obedience he offered him as a sacrifice to God on one of the mountains that he showed him.

footnotes.text: Clement's citation follows the Greek scriptural form and reads 'tribes of the earth' here rather than the more familiar English wording 'families of the earth.'
Or 'it was reckoned to him as righteousness.'
The phrase 'because of faith and hospitality' preserves Clement's wording and paraenetic emphasis on Abraham's concrete virtue toward others.

chapters.text:
Because of hospitality and godliness, Lot was saved from Sodom, when the whole surrounding region was judged by fire and sulfur. In this the Master made it plain that he does not abandon those who hope in him, but appoints those who turn aside to punishment and torment.

For when his wife went out with him, being of a different mind and not in harmony, she was set forth as a sign, so that she became a pillar of salt to this very day, in order that it might be known to all that the double-minded and those who doubt the power of God come to judgment and become a warning to all generations.

footnotes.text: Fire and sulfur echoes the judgment on Sodom in Genesis 19.
Or 'the wayward' for 'those who turn aside' (ἑτεροκλινεῖς).
'Double-minded' translates δίψυχοι, literally suggesting a divided inner life.

chapters.text: Because of faith and hospitality, Rahab the prostitute was saved. 2 For when spies had been sent out by Joshua son of Nun to Jericho, the king of the land learned that they had come to spy out their country, and he sent men to seize them, so that, once captured, they might be put to death. 3 So Rahab, being hospitable, welcomed them and hid them in the upper room under the stalks of flax. 4 But when the men from the king came and said, “The spies of our land came in to you; bring them out, for the king so orders,” she answered, “The men whom you seek did indeed come in to me, but they immediately departed and are going on the road,” while she pointed them in another direction. 5 And she said to the men, “I know for certain that the Lord God is giving this land to you, for the fear and dread of you has fallen upon those who dwell in it. Therefore, when it comes about that you take it, preserve me and my father’s house.” 6 And they said to her, “It shall be so, as you have spoken to us. But when you know that we are arriving, you shall gather all your people under your roof, and they will be preserved; for as many as are found outside the house will perish.” 7 And they added that they would give her a sign, that she should hang a scarlet cord from her house, making it clear that through the blood of the Lord there will be redemption for all who believe and hope in God. 8 You see, beloved, that in the woman there came to be not only faith, but also prophecy.

footnotes.text: Clement interprets Rahab's scarlet sign typologically: the visible scarlet marker points beyond the Joshua narrative to redemption through the Lord's blood.
The Greek at the end of the verse is textually awkward in this OCR witness; the sense is that Rahab misdirected the king's men by indicating another way.
That 'prophecy' was present in Rahab means Clement sees more than faithful action in her; he sees a prophetic sign in her words and in the scarlet token.

chapters.text: Let us therefore be humble, brothers, laying aside all arrogance and conceit and foolishness and fits of anger, and let us do what is written—for the Holy Spirit says: “Let not the wise man boast in his wisdom, nor the strong in his strength, nor the rich in his riches, but let the one who boasts boast in the Lord,” to seek him and to do judgment and righteousness—especially remembering the words of the Lord Jesus, which he spoke while teaching gentleness and patience. 2 For thus he said: “Show mercy, so that you may receive mercy. Forgive, so that it may be forgiven you. As you do, so it will be done to you. As you give, so it will be given to you. As you judge, so you will be judged. As you show kindness, so kindness will be shown to you. With the measure by which you measure, it will be measured to you.” 3 By this command and these charges let us strengthen ourselves so as to walk in obedience to his holy and fitting words, being humble-minded. For the holy word says: “Upon whom shall I look, except upon the gentle and quiet one, and the one who trembles at my words?”

footnotes.text: The citation combines wording known from Jeremiah 9:23–24 (LXX numbering differs) with the call to do judgment and righteousness.
The sayings in verse 2 echo material known from the Gospel tradition, especially forms similar to Matthew 5:7; 6:14; 7:2 and Luke 6:31, 36–38.
'Holy and fitting words' renders ἁγιοπρεπέσι λόγοις, literally words befitting holiness or sacred propriety.
The final citation echoes Isaiah 66:2, with 'gentle and quiet' reflecting Clement's wording here.

chapters.text: It is right, then, and holy, brothers, for us to become obedient to God rather than to follow those who, in arrogance and disorder, are leaders in hateful jealousy. For we will incur no ordinary harm, but rather great danger, if we recklessly hand ourselves over to the purposes of men who are bent on plunging us into strife and factions, so as to estrange us from what is good. Let us show kindness to ourselves according to the compassion and gentleness of the One who made us. For it is written: “The kind will inhabit the land, and the innocent will be left upon it; but those who transgress the law will be destroyed from it.” And again he says: “I saw the ungodly highly exalted and lifted up like the cedars of Lebanon; and I passed by, and behold, he was not there, and I sought his place, and I did not find it. Keep innocence and observe uprightness, because there is a remnant for a peaceable man.”

footnotes.text: Greek 'brothers' translates a masculine fraternal address (ἄνδρες ἀδελφοί).
'Hateful jealousy' could also be rendered 'detestable zeal'; here ζῆλος is taken negatively from the context of arrogance, disorder, strife, and factions.
The citations echo Ps 37:9, 35–37 (LXX numbering differs in some traditions).
'There is a remnant for a peaceable man' may also be understood as 'there is a future/posterity for a peaceable man.'

chapters.text: Therefore let us join ourselves to those who make peace with godliness, and not to those who want peace with hypocrisy. For somewhere it says, “This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far away from me.” And again, “With their mouth they were blessing, but with their heart they were cursing.” And again it says, “They loved him with their mouth, and with their tongue they lied to him; but their heart was not upright with him, nor were they found faithful in his covenant.” Because of this, let the deceitful lips that speak lawlessness against the righteous become mute. And again, “May the Lord destroy all deceitful lips, the boastful tongue, those who say, ‘We will make our tongue great; our lips are our own; who is lord over us?’ ‘Because of the misery of the poor and because of the groaning of the needy, now I will arise,’ says the Lord. ‘I will set him in safety; I will speak openly in him.’”

footnotes.text: Or 'let us cling to those who live at peace with godliness.'
'Were found faithful' may also be rendered 'were steadfast' or 'proved trustworthy.'
The wording echoes Psalmic imprecations against deceitful and arrogant speech.
The final clause is difficult. It may also be understood as 'I will act boldly in him' or, less likely, with a different nuance of the prepositional phrase.

chapters.text:
For the Messiah belongs to the humble-minded, not to those who throw themselves upon his fear. The scepter of the majesty of God, the Lord Jesus Messiah, did not come in the pomp of arrogance or pride, though he was able to do so, but in humility of mind, just as the Holy Spirit spoke concerning him. For it says:

“Lord, who has believed our report?
and to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?
We announced him before him,
like a child,
like a root in thirsty ground.
He has no form nor glory,
and we saw him,
and he had neither form nor beauty,
but his appearance was dishonored,
failing beyond the appearance of men;
a man being in plague and toil
and knowing how to bear weakness,
because his face is turned away;
he was dishonored and not esteemed.
This one bears our sins
and suffers pain on our behalf,
and we considered him to be in toil
and in plague and in affliction.
But he was wounded because of our sins
and has been weakened because of our lawless deeds.
The chastisement of our peace was upon him;
by his bruise we were healed.
All we like sheep went astray;
each man went astray in his own way.
And the Lord gave him over for our sins,
and he, because of being afflicted,
does not open his mouth.
Like a sheep he was led to slaughter,
and like a lamb before the one shearing it is voiceless,
so he does not open his mouth.
In his humiliation his judgment was taken away.
Who will describe his generation?
For his life is taken from the earth.
Because of the lawless deeds of my people he came to death.
And I will give the wicked in place of his burial,
and the rich in place of his death;
because he did no lawless deed,
nor was deceit found in his mouth.
And the Lord wills to cleanse him from the blow.
If you give for sin,
your soul will see a long-lived offspring.
And the Lord wills to take away from the toil of his soul,
to show him light and to form him with understanding,
to justify a righteous one who serves well many;
and he himself will bear their sins.
Because of this he will inherit many,
and he will divide the spoils of the strong,
because his soul was given over to death,
and he was counted among the lawless.
And he bore the sins of many,
and because of their sins he was given over.”

And again he himself says,
“But I am a worm and not a man,
a reproach of men and despised by the people.
All who see me mocked me;
they spoke with their lips,
they shook their head:
‘He hoped in the Lord; let him rescue him;
let him save him, because he delights in him.’”

You see, beloved men, what the pattern given to us is. For if the Lord so humbled himself, what should we do, we who through him have come under the yoke of his grace?

footnotes.text: The opening clause is textually and syntactically difficult. The sense conveyed is that the Messiah is aligned with humility, not with self-assertive intimidation before his fear.
The long central section is a citation of Isaiah 53 in Septuagintal form, with wording that differs at points from both the Hebrew text and familiar English Bible tradition.
'Pattern' translates ὑπογραμμός, a term for a model or copy set before someone for imitation.
The final exhortation turns the prophetic portrait of the suffering Lord into a moral summons for the church: humility is not only admired in him but required of those under his grace.

chapters.text: Let us become imitators also of those who went about in goatskins and sheepskins, proclaiming the coming of the Messiah. We mean Elijah and Elisha, and also Ezekiel, the prophets; and besides these, those who have received testimony. 2. Abraham received great testimony and was called the friend of God, and as he fixed his gaze on the glory of God he said in humility of mind, “But I am dust and ashes.” 3. And concerning Job it is thus written: “Now Job was righteous and blameless, true, God-fearing, and turning away from every evil thing.” 4. But he himself brings accusation against himself, saying, “No one is clean from defilement, not even if his life is but one day.” 5. Moses was called faithful in all his house, and through his service God judged Egypt by their plagues and torments; yet even he, though greatly glorified, did not become high-minded, but said, when an oracle was given to him at the bush, “Who am I, that you send me? But I am weak-voiced and slow of tongue.” 6. And again he says, “But I am vapor from a pot.”

footnotes.text: The clothing imagery recalls the scriptural description of prophetic hardship and may echo Hebrews 11:37.
The wording reflects a traditional scriptural citation about human impurity and brevity of life; the exact form differs from the standard text of Job in some manuscripts.
Or “steam from a pot.” The image emphasizes frailty and insubstantiality.

chapters.text:
And what shall we say about David, who has been borne witness to? To him God said, “I found a man according to my heart, David the son of Jesse; with everlasting mercy I anointed him.”

But he too says to God:

“Have mercy on me, O God, according to your great mercy,
and according to the abundance of your compassions blot out my lawless deed.
Wash me still more from my lawlessness,
and cleanse me from my sin.
For I know my lawlessness,
and my sin is before me continually.
Against you only I sinned,
and I did what is evil before you,
so that you may be justified in your words
and prevail when you are judged.
For behold, in lawless deeds I was conceived,
and in sins my mother nursed me.
For behold, you loved truth;
the hidden and secret things of your wisdom you made known to me.
You will sprinkle me with hyssop, and I will be cleansed;
you will wash me, and I will be made whiter than snow.
You will make me hear gladness and rejoicing;
the bones that have been humbled will rejoice.
Turn your face away from my sins,
and blot out all my lawless deeds.
Create in me a clean heart, O God,
and renew a right spirit in my inward parts.
Do not cast me away from your presence,
and do not take back your Holy Spirit from me.
Restore to me the rejoicing of your salvation,
and strengthen me with a ruling spirit.
I will teach the lawless your ways,
and the ungodly will turn back to you.
Deliver me from bloodguilt, O God, the God of my salvation.
My tongue will rejoice in your righteousness.
Lord, you will open my mouth,
and my lips will declare your praise.
For if you had desired sacrifice, I would have given it;
in whole burnt offerings you will not delight.
A sacrifice to God is a broken spirit;
a broken and humbled heart God will not despise.”

footnotes.text: The wording combines language known from scriptural testimony about David; Clement invokes David as one already publicly attested by God.
The Greek here reads 'my mother nursed me,' a form differing from the more familiar Psalm wording often rendered 'my mother conceived me.'
Or 'with a governing spirit' or 'with a noble spirit.' The Greek suggests a leading, ruling quality rather than merely inward freedom.
Literally 'from bloods,' an idiom commonly expressing bloodguilt.
'Broken' could also be rendered 'crushed' or, more interpretively, 'contrite.'

chapters.text:
Therefore, the humility and submissiveness of so many and such great people, so well attested, through obedience has made not only us better, but also the generations before us—those who received his oracles in holiness and truth.

2. Since, then, we have shared in many great and glorious deeds, let us press on toward the goal of peace that has been handed down to us from the beginning, and let us fix our gaze on the Father and Creator of the whole world, and cling to his magnificent and surpassing gifts of peace and acts of kindness.

3. Let us behold him in our mind, and let us look with the eyes of the soul into his patient purpose. Let us understand how free from anger he is toward all his creation.

footnotes.text: Or 'lowliness' for 'submissiveness'; the Greek term suggests a humble, yielding posture associated with obedience.
'His oracles' refers to divine utterances; 'his words' is a possible simpler rendering.
The phrase 'goal of peace' uses an athletic or directional image: the aim or mark toward which believers are to move.
Or 'with the soul's eyes.' The Greek preserves a vivid metaphor of inward spiritual perception.
'Free from anger' here highlights God's patient restraint toward creation in context; compare the emphasis on his 'patient purpose.'

chapters.text: The heavens, shaken by his governance, submit to him in peace. Day and night complete the course appointed by him, hindering one another in nothing. The sun and the moon and the choirs of stars, according to his ordinance, move in harmony, without any deviation, within the bounds assigned to them. The earth, bearing fruit according to his will, brings forth at the proper seasons abundant food for men and beasts and for all living things upon it, neither wavering nor altering anything of what has been decreed by him. The unsearchable depths of the abysses and the indescribable regions below are held together by the same ordinances. The basin of the boundless sea, gathered together by his workmanship into its reservoirs, does not pass beyond the barriers set around it, but as he ordered it, so it does. For he said, “Up to this point you shall come, and your waves shall be broken within you.” The ocean, impassable to men, and the worlds beyond it are directed by the decrees of the Master. The seasons of spring and summer and autumn and winter give way to one another in peace. The stations of the winds, each in its own season, fulfill their service without stumbling; and the ever-flowing springs, created for enjoyment and health, without fail supply their breasts to men for life. Even the smallest of living creatures hold their gatherings in harmony and peace. All these things the great Creator and Master of all has appointed to exist in peace and harmony, doing good to all things, but far beyond measure to us who have fled for refuge to his mercies through our Lord Jesus Messiah, to whom be the glory and the majesty for ever and ever. Amen.

footnotes.text: The quotation in verse 7 echoes Job 38:11.
“Worlds beyond it” reflects the plural κόσμoi and likely refers to realms or regions beyond the ocean in ancient cosmological language.
“Their breasts” preserves the Greek metaphor for springs nourishing human life.
Or “the indescribable judgments of the lower regions” for the difficult phrase in verse 5.

chapters.text: See to it, beloved, that his many good gifts do not become a judgment against us all, if we do not live in a manner worthy of him, and with one accord do what is good and pleasing before him. For somewhere it says, “The Spirit of the Lord is a lamp, searching the storerooms of the belly.” Let us see how near he is, and that none of our thoughts, nor the reasonings we carry on, has escaped his notice. Therefore it is right that we should not desert his will. Rather, let us offend foolish and senseless men, men who are puffed up and boast in the arrogance of their speech, than offend God. Let us have reverence for the Lord Jesus the Messiah, whose blood was given for us; let us respect those who lead us; let us honor the elders; let us train the younger in the discipline that comes from the fear of God; let us direct our women toward what is good. Let them show the lovely character of purity; let them demonstrate the sincere intention of their gentleness; let them make the moderation of their tongue evident through silence; let them extend their love not according to partialities, but equally to all who fear God in holiness. Let our children partake of the discipline that is in the Messiah. Let them learn what humility has strength before God, what pure love is able to do before God, how his fear is good and great and saves all who conduct themselves in it in holiness, with a pure mind. For he is a searcher of thoughts and intentions. His breath is in us, and whenever he wills, he will take it away.

footnotes.text: The citation echoes Proverbs 20:27. The Greek wording here reads literally, 'The Spirit of the Lord is a lamp, searching the storerooms of the belly.'
Or 'our wives' for 'our women'; the Greek term can bear either sense depending on context.
'Breath' here refers to the life-breath God gives and can remove at will, not necessarily to the Holy Spirit mentioned in the earlier citation.

chapters.text: And the faith that is in the Messiah confirms all these things. For he himself also, through the Holy Spirit, thus calls to us: “Come, children, listen to me; I will teach you the fear of the Lord. Who is the man who desires life, loving to see good days? Stop your tongue from evil, and your lips from speaking deceit. Turn away from evil and do good. Seek peace and pursue it. The eyes of the Lord are upon the righteous, and his ears are toward their petition; but the face of the Lord is against those who do evil, to wipe out their memory from the earth. The righteous man cried out, and the Lord heard him and delivered him from all his afflictions. Many are the afflictions of the righteous, and from all of them the Lord will deliver him.” Then: “Many are the blows of the sinner, but mercy will surround those who hope in the Lord.”

footnotes.text: The wording from this point is a citation of Psalm 34:11–17, with the final sentence echoing Psalm 32:10 in the Greek tradition.
Or possibly, 'faith in the Messiah confirms all these things.' The Greek phrase can be rendered more explicitly, but the main text keeps the source's compact form.
'Man' renders Greek ἄνθρωπος in a representative singular sense, not as excluding women.
'Blows' could also be rendered 'scourges' or 'lashes,' but the Greek can denote severe strokes or punishments more generally.

chapters.text:
The compassionate and altogether beneficent Father has tender affections for those who fear him, and gently and kindly gives his gracious gifts to those who come to him with a single-minded heart.

Therefore let us not be double-minded, nor let our soul be inwardly divided over his surpassing and glorious gifts. Far be from us this scripture, where it says, “Wretched are the double-minded, those who waver in their soul, who say, ‘These things we heard even in the days of our fathers, and look, we have grown old, and none of these things has happened to us.’”

You foolish ones, compare yourselves to a tree: take a vine. First it sheds its leaves, then a shoot appears, then a leaf, then a flower, and after these comes the unripe grape, then the full cluster appears. You see that in a little season the fruit of the tree comes to maturity.

Truly, quickly and suddenly his will shall be accomplished, as the scripture also bears witness, “Quickly he will come, and he will not delay,” and, “Suddenly the Lord will come to his temple, even the Holy One whom you expect.”

footnotes.text: The rare verb rendered 'be inwardly divided' is difficult; the sense is likely inner wavering or instability in parallel with 'be double-minded.'
The citation is traditionally associated with wording similar to James 1:8 and related early Christian paraenetic tradition, though Clement's form is not identical to the canonical text.
The closing scriptural catena echoes Habakkuk 2:3 and Malachi 3:1 in Septuagintal form.
Or 'await' instead of 'expect' for προσδοκᾶτε.

chapters.text: Let us consider, beloved, how the Master continually shows us that the resurrection that is going to happen will indeed be, of which he made the Lord Jesus Messiah the firstfruits by raising him from the dead. Let us observe, beloved, the resurrection that takes place in its appointed season. Day and night show us a resurrection: the night falls asleep, and the day rises; the day departs, and night comes on. Let us take the crops as an example: how and in what way does the seed come to be? The sower went out and cast each of the seeds into the ground, and when they fall into the ground, dry and bare, they decay; then from that decay the greatness of the Master's providence raises them up, and from one he increases many and brings forth fruit.

footnotes.text: Or 'the coming resurrection.' The Greek expression is emphatic, stressing the certainty of the future resurrection.
'Firstfruits' echoes biblical sacrificial and harvest language and presents Jesus Messiah as the initial instance and guarantee of the resurrection to come.
The image of night 'falling asleep' and day 'rising' is part of Clement's rhetorical analogy, not a literal cosmology.

chapters.text: Let us consider the remarkable sign that takes place in the eastern regions, that is, in the areas around Arabia. For there is a bird that is called the phoenix. This creature, being the only one of its kind, lives for five hundred years; and when it has now come near to the end of its life, it makes for itself a nest from frankincense and myrrh and the other spices. When the time has been fulfilled, it enters into it and dies. But as its flesh decays, a certain worm is produced, and being nourished from the moisture of the dead creature, it grows wings. Then, when it has become strong, it lifts up that nest in which are the bones of the one that came before, and carrying them it travels from the land of Arabia as far as Egypt, to the city called Heliopolis. And in daytime, in the sight of all, it flies to the altar of the sun and places them there, and so sets out back again. So then the priests inspect the records of the times, and they find that it has come when the five hundredth year has been completed.

footnotes.text: The phoenix was a well-known ancient bird tradition used as an example of renewal or return to life.
The Greek word rendered 'nest' can also denote an enclosed structure or chamber; the imagery here is of a self-made aromatic resting place for death and renewal.
Heliopolis means 'City of the Sun.'

chapters.text: Therefore, do we consider it a great and marvelous thing if the Maker of all things will bring about the resurrection of those who have served him in holiness, in the confidence of good faith, when even through a bird he shows us the greatness of his promise? 2. For somewhere he says, “And you will raise me up, and I will give thanks to you”; and, “I lay down and slept; I awoke, because you are with me.” 3. And again, Iob says, “And you will raise up this flesh of mine, which has endured all these things.”

footnotes.text: The 'bird' alludes to the phoenix example discussed in the surrounding context as an illustration of resurrection.
The citation formulas ('somewhere he says,' 'Iob says') introduce scriptural proof texts used to support the resurrection hope.
Or 'this body of mine' for 'this flesh of mine'; the Greek is more literally 'flesh.'

chapters.text: So then, let our souls be bound to this hope in the One who is faithful in his promises and righteous in his judgments. He who commanded us not to lie will much more not himself lie; for nothing is impossible with God, except to lie. Therefore let faith in him be rekindled in us, and let us understand that all things are near to him. By a word of his majesty he established all things, and by a word he is able to overturn them. Who will say to him, “What have you done?” Or who will withstand the might of his strength? When he wills, and as he wills, he will do all things, and not one of the things decreed by him shall ever pass away. All things are before him, and nothing has escaped his purpose. Since the heavens declare the glory of God, and the firmament proclaims the work of his hands, day pours forth speech to day, and night announces knowledge to night, and there are no words or utterances whose voices are not heard.

footnotes.text: The final sentence echoes Psalm 19:1–3(4 LXX), cited as scriptural support for God's all-seeing sovereignty and manifest glory in creation.
Or 'nothing will pass by from the things decreed by him'—that is, none of God's determinations will fail to stand.

chapters.text:
Therefore, since all things are seen and heard, let us fear him and leave behind the foul desires that belong to wicked deeds, so that by his mercy we may be sheltered from the judgments that are about to come.

2 For where can any one of us flee from his mighty hand? And what world will receive one of those who desert from him? For the Scripture says somewhere,

3 “Where shall I arrive, and where shall I hide from your face? If I go up into heaven, you are there; if I depart to the farthest parts of the earth, there is your right hand; if I make my bed in the depths, there is your Spirit.”

4 Where, then, can anyone go away, or where can he run off to, from the One who encompasses all things?

footnotes.text: The phrase 'those who desert from him' renders a term used for defectors or deserters, giving the warning a sharper force than a simple 'those who depart.'
'The Scripture says somewhere' introduces a citation echoing Psalm 139:7–10 (LXX 138:7–10), though Clement's wording is adapted rather than identical.
'From your face' is a literal rendering of a biblical idiom often translated 'from your presence.'
Or 'from the One who contains all things.' The Greek participle stresses God's all-encompassing presence.

chapters.text:
Therefore let us come near to him in holiness of soul, lifting up to him hands that are pure and undefiled, loving our gentle and compassionate Father, who has made us a chosen portion for himself.

2. For thus it is written:
“When the Most High divided the nations,
when he scattered the sons of Adam,
he fixed the boundaries of the nations
according to the number of the angels of God.
And the Lord’s portion became his people Jacob,
Israel the measuring-line of his inheritance.”

3. And in another place he says:
“Behold, the Lord takes for himself a nation from the midst of nations,
as a man takes the firstfruits of his threshing floor;
and from that nation the holy of holies shall come forth.”

footnotes.text: The citation reflects the Greek wording as given here, including 'according to the number of the angels of God.' Other textual traditions of Deuteronomy differ at this point.
'The holy of holies' is a literal rendering of the Greek phrase and may be understood in more than one way in this context.

chapters.text:
Therefore, since we are a holy portion, let us do all the things that belong to sanctification, fleeing slander, foul and unclean entanglements, drunkenness, seditions, and detestable desires, loathsome adultery, and hateful pride.

For God, he says, opposes the proud, but gives grace to the humble.

Let us therefore cling to those to whom grace has been given from God. Let us clothe ourselves with concord, being humble-minded and self-controlled, keeping ourselves far from every whispering and slander, being justified by works and not by words.

For he says, “He who says many things shall also hear in reply.” Or does the ready speaker think himself righteous?

“Blessed is one born of woman, short-lived. Do not become excessive in words.”

Let our praise be in God and not from ourselves, for God hates those who praise themselves.

Let the testimony of our good deed be given by others, just as it was given to our righteous fathers.

Boldness and arrogance and recklessness belong to those cursed by God; but gentleness and humility and meekness are found among those blessed by God.

footnotes.text: The citation echoes Proverbs 3:34 in the form also used in James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5.
These lines appear to echo Job tradition, especially themes found in Job 11:2-3 and 14:1.
Or 'being shown righteous by works and not by words.' The Greek verb can denote demonstrated righteousness in conduct.
The term rendered 'seditions' can also be understood as disruptive 'innovations' or factional upheavals.

chapters.text: Let us therefore cling to his blessing, and let us see what the ways of blessing are. Let us unfold the things that have happened from the beginning. For what reason was our father Abraham blessed? Was it not because he practiced righteousness and truth through faith? Isaac, knowing what was to come with confidence, gladly allowed himself to be led as a sacrifice. Jacob, with humility, departed from his land because of his brother and went to Laban and served, and to him was given the twelve-sceptered people of Israel.

footnotes.text: Or 'by faith.' The Greek phrase διὰ πίστεως can be rendered either way in English; the sense remains instrumental.
The wording highlights Isaac's willing submission in the binding narrative; compare Genesis 22.
Literally 'the twelve-sceptered [thing/people] of Israel,' an elevated expression referring to Israel as constituted in twelve tribal rulerships.

chapters.text: Whoever sincerely considers each thing one by one will recognize the greatness of the gifts given by him. For from him are priests and Levites, all who minister at the altar of God; from him is the Lord Jesus according to the flesh; from him are kings and rulers and leaders according to Judah; and his remaining tribes are not in small glory, just as God promised, saying, “Your seed will be as the stars of heaven.” Therefore all were glorified and made great, not through themselves or through their works or through the righteous deeds that they accomplished, but through his will. And so we also, having been called through his will in Messiah Jesus, are not justified through ourselves, nor through our own wisdom or understanding or godliness or works that we have done in holiness of heart, but through faith, by which the Almighty God justified all who have been from the beginning. To him be the glory forever and ever. Amen.

footnotes.text: Or 'are in no little glory.' The Greek uses a litotes, an understated way of expressing significant honor.
'According to the flesh' likely refers to Jesus' descent in human lineage from the patriarchal line, especially Judah; compare similar early Christian genealogical language.
Some may prefer 'Christ Jesus' here. We render Χριστῷ as 'Messiah' where the title's meaning remains active.

chapters.text: What then shall we do, brothers? Shall we grow idle in doing good and abandon love? May the Master never allow this to happen among us. Rather, let us hasten with earnestness and eagerness to accomplish every good work. For the Creator and Master of all himself rejoices in his works. For by his exceedingly great might he established the heavens, and by his incomprehensible understanding he ordered them. He also separated the earth from the water that surrounds it and fixed it upon the secure foundation of his own purpose. And the living creatures that move about on it he commanded to exist by his own ordinance. Having prepared the sea and the living creatures in it, he enclosed it by his own power. Above all, with his holy and undefiled hands he formed man, the most excellent and truly great being in understanding, as the imprint of his own image. For thus God says, “Let us make man according to our image and according to our likeness.” And God made man; male and female he made them. So then, when he had finished all these things, he praised them and blessed them and said, “Increase and multiply.” Let us see, then, that all the righteous were adorned with good works, and that the Lord himself, adorning himself with good works, rejoiced. Having, then, this pattern, let us come eagerly to his will. With all our strength let us work the work of righteousness.

footnotes.text: Or 'with fervor and readiness' for 'with earnestness and eagerness.'
The wording echoes Gen 1:26–28.
'Man' here carries a representative sense for humanity in the creation account, as shown by the following phrase 'male and female he made them.'
Or 'example' for 'pattern'; the Greek term can denote a model set before someone for imitation.

chapters.text:
The good worker receives with boldness the bread of his labor, but the sluggish and negligent man cannot look his employer in the eye.

Therefore we must be eager for doing good, for from him are all things. For he says beforehand to us, “Behold, the Lord, and his reward is before his face, to repay each one according to his work.” Therefore he urges us, who believe on him with our whole heart, not to be idle or negligent in any good work.

Let our boasting and our boldness be in him. Let us submit ourselves to his will. Let us consider the whole multitude of his angels, how they stand by and minister to his will. For the Scripture says, “Ten thousand times ten thousand stood by him, and thousands of thousands ministered to him,” and they cried out, “Holy, holy, holy is the Lord Sabaoth; all creation is full of his glory.”

So then, let us also, gathered together in concord to the same place, cry out to him earnestly with one conscience, as from one mouth, so that we may become sharers in his great and glorious promises. For he says, “Eye has not seen, and ear has not heard, and it has not arisen upon the heart of man, the things that he prepared for those who wait for him.”

footnotes.text: Or, more interpretively, “the Lord of hosts.” The Greek text here reads “Sabaoth,” preserving the Hebrew-derived title.
Literally, “with one conscience,” emphasizing shared moral awareness as well as unity.
Or “for those who endure for him.” The wording likely echoes the scriptural theme of waiting expectantly for God, though the Greek verb can also stress perseverance.
Compare Isaiah 64:4 and 1 Corinthians 2:9 for closely related wording.

chapters.text: How blessed and marvelous are the gifts of God, beloved: life in immortality, splendor in righteousness, truth in boldness, faith in confidence, self-control in holiness. And all these things fall within the grasp of our understanding. What then, therefore, are the things prepared for those who patiently endure for him? The Creator and Father of the ages, the all-holy one himself, knows their measure and their beauty. Therefore let us strive to be found in the number of those who patiently endure him, so that we may share in the gifts that have been promised. But how will this be, beloved? If our mind has been fixed faithfully on God, if we seek out the things pleasing and acceptable to him, if we accomplish the things that belong to his blameless purpose and follow the way of truth, casting away from ourselves all unrighteousness and wickedness, greed, strife, malicious habits and deceits, whisperings and slanders, hatred of God, pride and arrogance, empty glory and lack of hospitality. For those who practice these things are hateful to God; and not only those who practice them, but also those who approve of them. For the Scripture says, “But to the sinner God said, Why do you recount my righteous requirements, and take up my covenant upon your mouth? Yet you hated discipline, and cast my words behind you. If you saw a thief, you ran with him, and you placed your share with adulterers. Your mouth overflowed with evil, and your tongue kept weaving deceit. Sitting down, you were slandering your brother, and against the son of your mother you were laying a stumbling block. These things you did, and I kept silent; you supposed, lawless one, that I would be like you. I will rebuke you and set you before your face. Understand these things, then, you who forget God, lest he seize like a lion, and there be no one to rescue. A sacrifice of praise will glorify me, and there is the way by which I will show him the salvation of God.”

footnotes.text: The closing citation echoes Psalm 50:16–23 LXX (Psalm 50 in Greek numbering; Psalm 51 in some English traditions differs because of numbering conventions). The wording here follows Clement's Greek citation rather than a standardized English Psalter form.
