'Father of the ages' renders the plural αἰώνων more literally than 'Father of eternity.'
The phrase rendered 'patiently endure him' is syntactically compressed in Greek and may also be understood along the lines of 'wait for him' or 'persevere in relation to him.'

chapters.text:
This is the way, beloved, in which we found our salvation: Jesus the Messiah, the high priest of our offerings, the protector and helper of our weakness.

Through him we fix our gaze on the heights of the heavens; through him we behold as in a mirror his spotless and most exalted countenance; through him the eyes of our heart were opened; through him our foolish and darkened mind springs up into the light; through him the Master willed that we should taste immortal knowledge—he who, being the radiance of his majesty, is by so much greater than the angels as he has inherited a more excellent name.

For it is written thus:
“He makes his angels winds,
and his ministers a flame of fire.”

But concerning his Son it says thus:
“You are my Son;
today I have begotten you.
Ask of me, and I will give you the nations as your inheritance,
and the ends of the earth as your possession.”

And again he says to him:
“Sit at my right hand,
until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet.”

Who then are the enemies? The vile, and those who set themselves against his will.

footnotes.text: Or 'spirits' in the citation, since the Greek word can mean either 'winds' or 'spirits.'
The wording in this section echoes themes and phrases known from Hebrews 1:3–7 and Psalm citations used in early Christian christological argument.

chapters.text:
Therefore let us serve as soldiers, brothers, with all earnestness under his blameless commands. Let us consider those who serve as soldiers under our commanders: how orderly, how readily, how submissively they carry out what is commanded. Not all are prefects, nor tribunes, nor centurions, nor commanders of fifty, nor so on; but each, in his own rank, carries out the orders given by the king and by the commanders.

The great cannot exist without the small, nor the small without the great. There is a certain blending in all things, and in this there is usefulness. Let us take our body as an example: the head is nothing without the feet; likewise the feet are nothing without the head. Even the smallest members of our body are necessary and useful to the whole body. But all work together in harmony and make use of one submission, so that the whole body may be preserved.

footnotes.text: The term rendered 'readily' may also be understood as 'obediently' or 'compliantly'; the sequence emphasizes disciplined responsiveness under authority.
'There is a certain blending in all things' expresses the interrelation of different parts rather than erasing their distinctions.
Literally, 'all breathe together'; the image is of the body's parts acting in concert under one ordered submission.
Compare the body analogy in 1 Corinthians 12:12–27, where many members are necessary to one body.

chapters.text: Therefore let our whole body be held together in Messiah Jesus, and let each one be subject to his neighbor, according as he was appointed in his own gift. 2. Let the strong take care of the weak, and let the weak respect the strong. Let the rich supply the poor, and let the poor give thanks to God, because he gave him one through whom his lack may be filled up. Let the wise show his wisdom not in words, but in good works. Let the humble-minded not bear witness to himself, but let him allow himself to be borne witness to by another. Let the one who is pure in the flesh not boast, knowing that it is another who supplies him self-control. 3. Let us therefore consider, brothers, from what material we came to be, what and who we were when we entered into the world, from what tomb and darkness the one who formed us and created us brought us into his world, having prepared his good gifts before we were born. 4. Since, then, we have all these things from him, we ought in every way to give thanks to him, to whom be the glory for the ages of ages. Amen.

footnotes.text: Or 'be joined together' or 'be knit together' for the opening exhortation about the whole body in Messiah Jesus.
'Pure in the flesh' may especially imply bodily chastity, but the Greek expression is broader than a narrowly technical term.

The clause 'because he gave him one through whom his lack may be filled up' is syntactically compressed; the sense is likely that God has provided the poor person with someone through whom his need is supplied.
Literally, 'for the ages of ages,' a traditional doxological expression.

chapters.text: Foolish and senseless and stupid and untaught men mock us and sneer at us, wanting to exalt themselves in their own thoughts. For what can a mortal do? Or what strength belongs to one born of earth? For it is written, “No form was before my eyes; rather, I was hearing a breath and a voice: What then? Shall a mortal be clean before the Lord? Or shall a man be blameless because of his works, if he does not trust even his own servants, and has perceived something crooked even in his angels? Even the heavens are not clean before him. How much less those who dwell in houses of clay, from which we ourselves also are from the same clay. He struck them like a moth, and from morning until evening they are no more; because they are unable to help themselves, they perished. He breathed on them, and they died, because they had no wisdom. But call out, if anyone will listen to you, or if you will see any of the holy angels; for anger kills the foolish man, and jealousy puts the one gone astray to death. And I have seen fools taking root, but immediately their dwelling was consumed. May their sons be far from safety; may they be crushed at the doors of inferiors, and there will be no one to rescue them. For what things you prepared for them, the righteous will eat, but they themselves will not be delivered from evils.”

footnotes.text: The citation in this chapter draws on Job language in a form not identical to standard English versions of Job 4–5.
'Servants' translates παῖδες here in the sense of attendants; 'children' is a possible lexical alternative but fits the context less well.
'Jealousy' could also be rendered 'envy' or, less likely in this context, 'zeal.'
The wording behind 'their dwelling was consumed' is difficult; the Greek term can also suggest manner of life or estate.

chapters.text: Therefore, since these things are plain to us, and since we have peered into the depths of the divine knowledge, we ought to do everything in order that the Master commanded to be carried out at the appointed times. He commanded that both the offerings and the ministries be performed, and not happen casually or disorderly, but at fixed times and hours. And where and through whom he wants them to be performed, he himself determined by his supreme will, so that, with all things being done in holiness, they might be acceptable with approval to his will. Therefore those who make their offerings at the appointed times that have been prescribed are both acceptable and blessed; for by following the lawful ordinances of the Master they do not go astray. For to the high priest his own ministries have been given, and to the priests their own place has been assigned, and upon Levites their own services are laid; the layman is bound by the layman's ordinances.

footnotes.text: Or 'well-pleasing and acceptable to his will.' The Greek expression is emphatic, combining favorable regard and acceptability.
The verb can also mean 'do not miss the mark' or 'do not transgress.'
Clement appeals to the ordered roles of Israel's worship—high priest, priests, Levites, and layman—as an analogy for proper order in the community.

chapters.text:
Let each of us, brothers, in his own appointed rank be pleasing to God, being in a good conscience, not overstepping the fixed rule of his own ministry, with dignity.

2. For the sacrifices of continual offering, or of prayers, or concerning sin and transgression, are not offered everywhere, brothers, but only in Jerusalem; and even there they are not offered in every place, but before the sanctuary, at the altar, after the offering has been inspected by the high priest and the ministers already mentioned.

footnotes.text: Or 'order' or 'station' for 'appointed rank' (τάγματι), emphasizing an assigned place within an ordered community.
The phrase 'after the offering has been inspected' renders a rare verb for examining a sacrifice for acceptability before offering.
Clement's argument echoes the ordered pattern of Israel's worship to support disciplined conduct within the church.

chapters.text: The apostles were given the good news for us from the Lord Jesus Messiah, and Jesus the Messiah was sent out from God. So then the Messiah is from God, and the apostles are from the Messiah; therefore both came about in good order from the will of God. Therefore, after receiving instructions, and having been fully assured through the resurrection of our Lord Jesus Messiah, and having been confirmed in the word of God, they went out with the full assurance of the Holy Spirit, proclaiming the good news that the kingdom of God is about to come. So, preaching through regions and cities, they appointed their firstfruits, after testing them by the Spirit, to be overseers and deacons for those who were going to believe. And this was not something new, for indeed it had been written about overseers and deacons from very ancient times. For somewhere the Scripture says, “I will appoint their overseers in righteousness, and their deacons in faith.”

footnotes.text: Or 'having been entrusted with the word of God' for πιστωθέντες ἐν τῷ λόγῳ τοῦ θεοῦ; the phrase may emphasize either confirmation in, or entrustment with, God's word.
'Overseers' translates ἐπίσκοποι and avoids importing later assumptions attached to the English word 'bishops.'
The closing citation echoes Isaiah 60:17 in a form adapted in early Christian usage.
'In faith' may also be understood as 'in faithfulness.'

chapters.text:
And what is remarkable if those entrusted in Messiah by God appointed the persons mentioned before to such a work? For even the blessed and faithful attendant Moses, in all his house, recorded in the sacred books all the things commanded to him; and the rest of the prophets followed him, bearing witness together with the ordinances laid down by him. For when jealousy arose concerning the priesthood and the tribes were in faction over which of them was adorned with the glorious name, he ordered the twelve tribal leaders to bring him staffs, each inscribed according to the name of its tribe. And taking them, he bound them and sealed them with the rings of the tribal leaders, and placed them in the tent of witness upon the table of God. And after shutting the tent, he sealed the keys in the same way, and also the staffs, and said to them, “Men, brothers, the tribe whose staff shall sprout—this one God has chosen to serve as priest and to minister to him.”

Then when morning came, he called together all Israel, the six hundred thousand men, and showed the seals to the tribal leaders; and he opened the tent of witness and brought out the staffs. And the staff of Aaron was found not only to have sprouted, but even to be bearing fruit. What do you think, beloved? Did not Moses know beforehand that this was going to happen? Certainly he knew. But so that there might not be disorder in Israel, he acted in this way, so that the name of the true and only God might be glorified—to whom be the glory forever and ever. Amen.

footnotes.text: Or 'those who believed in Messiah through God.' The Greek passive πιστευθέντες can be understood differently, but the context of appointment favors 'entrusted.'
'Tent of witness' echoes the biblical designation for the tabernacle in the wilderness; traditional English often has 'tabernacle of testimony.'
See Numbers 17 for the episode of Aaron's staff budding, to which Clement appeals as a precedent for divinely confirmed priestly order.
'Men, brothers' reflects the Greek Ἄνδρες ἀδελφοί, a direct and formal address; it is retained for source accuracy.

chapters.text: And our apostles knew through our Lord Jesus Messiah that there would be strife over the name of the overseer’s office. Therefore, for this reason, since they had received complete foreknowledge, they appointed the men already mentioned, and afterward they gave a further provision, so that if they should fall asleep, other approved men might succeed to their ministry. Therefore we consider it unjust that those who were appointed by them, or afterward by other reputable men with the whole church consenting, and who have served the flock of the Messiah blamelessly, with humility, quietly, and without arrogance, and who for a long time have been well attested by all—such men should be removed from the ministry. For it will be no small sin for us if we cast out from the overseer’s office those who have offered its gifts blamelessly and devoutly. Blessed are the elders who went on before, who had a timely and complete departure; for they do not fear lest someone remove them from the place appointed to them. For we see that you have displaced some who were conducting themselves well from the ministry that had been honorably and blamelessly assigned to them.

footnotes.text: Or 'over the office of oversight.' The Greek ἐπισκοπή can denote oversight or the office connected with it.
'Fall asleep' is a traditional Christian euphemism for death, retained here to preserve the text’s cadence and theological texture.
The phrase 'offered its gifts' reflects sacrificial language in the Greek, presenting church office in liturgical-service terms rather than merely administrative ones.
'Elders' translates πρεσβύτεροι; some readers may prefer the transliteration 'presbyters.'

chapters.text:
You are contentious, brothers, and zealous about the things that pertain to salvation. You have bent down to look into the holy Scriptures, the true Scriptures, those given through the Holy Spirit. You know that nothing unjust or falsified has been written in them; you will not find righteous people cast off by holy men. The righteous were persecuted, but by the lawless; they were imprisoned, but by the unholy; they were stoned by the outlawed; they were killed by those who were defiled and unjust, through intolerable jealousy. While suffering these things, they endured nobly.

For what shall we say, brothers? Was Daniel thrown into a den of lions by those who feared God? Or were Hananiah and Azariah and Mishael brought down into a furnace of fire by those who practiced the magnificent and glorious religion of the Most High? May this never be! Who then were the ones who did these things? The hateful, and those full of every evil, were stirred up to such a pitch of wrath that they subjected to abuse those who served God in a holy and blameless purpose, not knowing that the Most High is champion and defender of those who worship his all-virtuous name with a pure conscience—to whom be the glory forever and ever. Amen.

But those who endure in confidence inherited glory and honor, and were exalted; and they became enrolled by God in their memorial forever and ever. Amen.

footnotes.text: The phrase rendered 'through intolerable jealousy' could also be translated 'by unbearable envy.'
The repeated θρησκ- wording is preserved with 'practiced ... religion' to reflect the Greek's emphasis on outward religious devotion.
Daniel in the lions' den: Dan 6. Hananiah, Azariah, and Mishael in the fiery furnace: Dan 3.
'In their memorial' may evoke the biblical idea of a divine record of remembrance, though the Greek here does not explicitly say 'book.'

chapters.text:
Therefore, brothers, we too must attach ourselves to such examples. For it is written, “Attach yourselves to the holy ones, because those who attach themselves to them will be made holy.” And again, in another place it says, “With an innocent man you will be innocent, and with the chosen one you will be chosen, and with the crooked you will become twisted.” Let us therefore attach ourselves to the innocent and righteous, and these are God’s chosen ones.

Why are there quarrels and outbursts of anger and divisions and schisms and even war among you? Do we not have one God and one Messiah and one Spirit of grace poured out upon us, and one calling in Messiah? Why do we tear apart and split up the members of the Messiah, and rise in revolt against our own body, and come to such a pitch of madness that we forget we are members of one another?

Remember the words of Jesus our Lord. For he said, “Woe to that man: it would have been good for him if he had not been born, rather than that he should cause one of my chosen ones to stumble. It would have been better for him to have a millstone hung around him and to be sunk into the sea, than that he should turn one of my chosen ones aside.” Your schism has turned many aside, cast many into discouragement, many into doubt, and all of us into grief; and your factional strife still continues.

footnotes.text: The citation “Attach yourselves to the holy ones...” is not a verbatim match to a surviving Old Testament verse as we have it; Clement may be citing a textual form, a conflated scriptural tradition, or a traditional saying.
“With an innocent man you will be innocent...” echoes scriptural language found in the Greek Psalms tradition; the final clause intensifies the warning that moral company shapes moral character.
“One Spirit of grace poured out upon us” likely evokes biblical outpouring language for God’s Spirit and underscores the shared divine gift that makes schism especially grievous.
Clement’s saying of Jesus resembles Gospel warnings about causing “little ones” to stumble, but here reads “one of my chosen ones,” a form worth preserving rather than harmonizing.

chapters.text: Take up the letter of the blessed Paul the apostle. What did he first write to you at the beginning of the gospel? In truth, he wrote to you in the Spirit about himself and Cephas and Apollos, because even then you had formed factions. But that factionalism brought less sin upon you; for you were aligning yourselves with apostles of approved testimony, and with a man tested by them. But now consider who it is that has perverted you and diminished the dignity of your widely spoken-of brotherly love. It is shameful, beloved—very shameful, and unworthy of conduct in the Messiah—that it should be heard that the very steadfast and ancient church of the Corinthians is in revolt against the elders because of one or two persons. And this report has reached not only us, but also those who are of a different mind from us, so that blasphemies are brought upon the name of the Lord because of your foolishness, while you also work danger for yourselves.

footnotes.text: The reference is to Paul's earlier letter to the Corinthians, especially the opening discussion of party divisions (compare 1 Corinthians 1:10–12).
Cephas is Peter.
Or 'he wrote to you spiritually.' The Greek adverb can be taken more generally, but the main text preserves the likely sense of Spirit-shaped admonition.
'Brotherly love' renders a familial term for mutual affection within the believing community.
Or 'is rebelling' / 'is in dissension.' The Greek verb is stronger than a mild disagreement.

chapters.text:
Let us therefore put this away quickly, and let us fall down before the Master and weep, imploring him that, having become gracious, he may be reconciled to us and restore us to the pure conduct befitting our solemn brotherly love.

For a gate of righteousness has been opened—this very gate into life—as it is written: “Open to me the gates of righteousness; entering through them, I will give thanks to the Lord.” “This is the gate of the Lord; the righteous will enter through it.”

Therefore, though many gates stand open, this is the gate in righteousness—the one in the Messiah. Blessed are all who entered through it and who direct their course in holiness and righteousness, carrying out all things without turmoil.

Let a man be faithful; let him be able to expound knowledge; let him be wise in discerning words; let him be pure in works. Yet by so much the more he ought to be humble, by as much as he seems to be greater, and to seek what benefits all, and not what is his own.

footnotes.text: The citation echoes Psalm 118:19–20 (LXX 117:19–20).
Greek 'in the Messiah'; many English readers will recognize the traditional rendering 'in Christ.'
The Greek uses a masculine singular form ('a man') in a representative paraenetic sense.

chapters.text: Whoever has love in the Messiah, let him do the Messiah’s commandments. Who is able to explain the bond of God’s love? Who is sufficient to tell the greatness of its beauty? The height to which love lifts us is beyond telling. Love joins us to God; love covers a multitude of sins; love endures all things, is patient in all things. There is nothing coarse in love, nothing arrogant. Love has no schism; love does not stir up faction; love does all things in concord. In love all the elect of God were made complete; apart from love, nothing is pleasing to God. In love the Master received us: because of the love that he had toward us, Jesus Messiah our Lord gave his blood for us by the will of God, and his flesh for our flesh and his life for our lives.

footnotes.text: Or 'in Christ.' The Greek title Χριστός is rendered 'Messiah' here to preserve its meaning as a title.
'Covers a multitude of sins' echoes scriptural language also found in Prov 10:12 and Jas 5:20; compare 1 Pet 4:8.
Or 'his soul for our souls.' The Greek ψυχή can mean 'life' or 'soul'; here 'life' best fits the sacrificial parallelism.

chapters.text: See, beloved, how great and marvelous love is, and of its perfection there is no explanation. Who is sufficient to be found in it, except those whom God may count worthy? Therefore let us beg and ask from his mercy that we may be found in love, without human partiality, blameless. All the generations from Adam until this day have passed away, but those who were perfected in love according to the grace of God have a place among the godly, and they will be revealed in the visitation of the kingdom of the Messiah. For it is written, “Enter into your inner chambers for a very little while, until my wrath and anger pass by; and I will remember a good day, and I will raise you from your tombs.” Blessed are we, beloved, if we do the commandments of God in the harmony of love, so that through love our sins may be forgiven us. For it is written, “Blessed are those whose lawless deeds were forgiven, and whose sins were covered; blessed is the man whose sin the Lord will certainly not reckon, and in whose mouth there is no deceit.” This blessing, then, came upon those chosen by God through Jesus Messiah our Lord, to whom be the glory forever and ever. Amen.

footnotes.text: The phrase 'visitation of the kingdom of the Messiah' may also be understood as the appearing or eschatological manifestation of the kingdom.
The citation echoes Isa 26:20 and combines language resembling resurrection hope; the wording here follows Clement's form of the citation.
Or 'in unity of love' for 'in the harmony of love.'
Quoted from Ps 32:1–2(31:1–2 LXX), with the final clause reflecting the Greek scriptural form familiar in early Christian usage.

chapters.text: Therefore, whatever things we have done wrong and committed through any schemes of the adversary, let us ask that they be forgiven us. And those who became leaders of revolt and division ought to consider the common good of our hope. For those who live as citizens with fear and love desire rather to fall into mistreatment themselves than to bring it upon their neighbors; indeed, they would rather bear condemnation of themselves than of the harmony that has been handed down to us well and rightly. For it is good for a man to confess concerning his transgressions rather than to harden his heart, just as the heart of those who rebelled against Moses, the attendant of God, was hardened—men whose judgment became plain. For they went down alive into Hades, and death will shepherd them. Pharaoh and his army and all the rulers of Egypt, both their chariots and those who rode in them, were sunk into the Red Sea and perished for no other cause than this: their senseless hearts were hardened after the signs and wonders had taken place in the land of Egypt through Moses, the attendant of God.

footnotes.text: Or 'servant of God' for 'attendant of God'; the Greek uses θεράπων, a more distinctive term than the common word for servant/slave.
'Death will shepherd them' echoes the wording of Psalm 49:14(15 LXX), preserving the scriptural cadence of the citation.
The phrase rendered 'schemes of the adversary' is somewhat elliptical in Greek and may also be understood more generally as influences or devices belonging to the opposing one.

chapters.text: The Master of all is without need, brothers; he has need of nothing except that confession be made to him. For the chosen David says, “I will confess to the Lord, and it will please him more than a young bull bringing forth horns and hoofs. Let the poor see and be glad.” And again he says, “Offer to God a sacrifice of praise, and pay your vows to the Most High; and call upon me in the day of your affliction, and I will deliver you, and you will glorify me.” For a sacrifice to God is a broken spirit.

footnotes.text: Or 'thanksgiving be offered to him' or 'praise be made to him'; the Greek verb can carry the sense of confession, acknowledgment, thanksgiving, or praise in biblical usage.
The quotations in this chapter draw on Psalmic language, especially Psalm 68:31(32 LXX) and Psalm 49:14-15, 19(50:14-15, 17 MT/LXX numbering differs).

chapters.text:
For you know the holy Scriptures—and know them well, beloved—and you have bent closely over the sayings of God. Therefore we write these things for your remembrance.

2. For when Moses had gone up onto the mountain and had spent forty days and forty nights in fasting and humiliation, God said to him, “Moses, Moses, come down quickly from here, because your people, whom you brought out of the land of Egypt, have acted lawlessly. They have quickly turned aside from the way that I commanded them; they have made molten images for themselves.”

3. And the Lord said to him, “I have spoken to you once and twice, saying, ‘I have seen this people, and behold, it is stiff-necked. Leave me, that I may utterly destroy them, and I will blot out their name from under heaven; and I will make you into a nation great and marvelous and far greater than this one.’”

4. And Moses said, “By no means, Lord. Forgive the sin of this people—or else blot me out from the book of the living.”

5. O great love! O unsurpassable perfection! A servant speaks boldly to his Lord, asks forgiveness for the multitude, or even pleads to be blotted out with them.

footnotes.text: The verb rendered 'have bent closely over' pictures stooping or leaning over the divine words in careful attention.
Or 'the book of life'; the Greek more literally says 'the book of the living.'
'Servant' renders θεράπων, an honorable term for one in devoted attendance, not the more common δοῦλος.
Clement is recalling the episode of Israel's sin with the calf and Moses' intercession; compare Exodus 32:7-32 and Deuteronomy 9:9-14.

chapters.text: Who then among you is noble? Who is compassionate? Who is fully assured in love? Let him say, ‘If because of me there is faction and strife and divisions, I withdraw, I depart wherever you wish, and I will do what is commanded by the multitude. Only let the flock of the Messiah live in peace with the elders who have been appointed.’ The man who has done this will obtain for himself great honor in the Messiah, and every place will receive him. For the earth is the Lord’s, and its fullness. These things those who live out the unrepented citizenship of God have done and will do.

footnotes.text: Or 'schisms' for 'divisions'; the Greek term can carry specifically ecclesial overtones.
'The multitude' may refer to the gathered congregation or the larger body of believers, not merely a crowd in the modern sense.
Psalm 24:1.
The phrase 'the unrepented citizenship of God' is difficult. Possible senses include 'the irrevocable commonwealth of God' or 'the way of life in God's commonwealth that brings no regret.'

chapters.text:
And so, to bring forward examples even from the nations: many kings and rulers, when some pestilential crisis had arisen, being instructed by oracles, handed themselves over to death so that through their own blood they might rescue their citizens. Many departed from their own cities so that they might no longer be torn by faction.

We know of many among us who have handed themselves over to chains so that they might ransom others. Many handed themselves over into slavery, and after receiving the price paid for them, they fed others.

Many women, strengthened through the grace of God, accomplished many manly deeds.

The blessed Ioudith, when the city was under siege, asked from the elders that she be permitted to go out into the camp of the foreigners. So then, giving herself over to danger, she went out because of love for her homeland and for the people who were under siege, and the Lord delivered Holofernes into the hand of a woman.

And no less also Esther, perfect according to faith, put herself in danger so that she might rescue the twelve-tribed people of Israel, who were about to perish. For through fasting and humiliation she entreated the all-seeing Master, God of the ages; and he, seeing the humility of her soul, rescued the people for whose sake she had faced danger.

footnotes.text: Or 'a plague-stricken time.' The Greek describes a deadly public crisis, likely involving pestilence or calamity.
Literally 'manly deeds.' Clement uses gendered language to commend the courage of these women.
Ioudith refers to Judith of the deuterocanonical book of Judith.
Or 'perfect in faith' / 'complete in faith.' The Greek phrase is concise and somewhat idiomatic.
Esther alludes especially to the Greek Esther tradition, with its emphasis on fasting and humble petition.

chapters.text:
Therefore let us also intercede concerning those who are caught in some transgression, so that gentleness and humility may be given to them, for them to yield not to us, but to the will of God. For in this way their remembrance before God and the saints will be fruitful and complete, with compassion.

Let us accept discipline, at which no one ought to be resentful, beloved. The admonition that we give to one another is good and exceedingly beneficial, for it binds us to the will of God. For thus says the holy word:

“The Lord surely disciplined me,
and he did not hand me over to death.”

“For the Lord disciplines the one whom he loves,
and he scourges every son whom he receives.”

“For,” it says, “the righteous one will discipline me in mercy and will reprove me,
but let not the oil of sinners anoint my head.”

And again it says:

“Blessed is the man whom the Lord has reproved;
and do not reject the admonition of the Almighty.
For he causes pain, and then restores again;
he struck, and his hands healed.
Six times he will deliver you from distresses,
and in the seventh no evil will touch you.
In famine he will rescue you from death,
and in war he will free you from the hand of the sword.
He will hide you from the scourge of the tongue,
and you will certainly not fear evils when they come.
You will laugh at the unrighteous and lawless,
and you will certainly not fear wild beasts,
for wild beasts will be at peace with you.
Then you will know that your house will be at peace,
and the condition of your tent will certainly not go wrong.
And you will know that your seed will be many,
and your children like the abundant grass of the field.
And you will come to the grave like ripe grain harvested in its season,
or like a heap from the threshing floor gathered in at the proper time.”

See, beloved, how great a protection there is for those who are disciplined by the Master. For, being a good Father, he disciplines us so that we may receive mercy through his holy discipline.

footnotes.text: Or 'their mention before God and the saints will be fruitful and complete with compassion.' The Greek phrase is compact and may refer either to the community's remembering them or to their being remembered favorably.
The citation echoes Psalm 141:5(140:5 LXX). The contrast is between righteous correction and the flattering 'oil' of sinners.
This extended citation draws chiefly from Job 5:17-26 in Septuagintal form.
'Master' renders Greek despotes, distinguished here from kyrios ('Lord') in the preceding citations.

chapters.text:
Therefore, you who laid the foundation of the uprising, submit yourselves to the elders and be disciplined unto repentance, bending the knees of your heart. Learn to submit, putting away the arrogant and proud self-will of your tongue. For it is better for you to be found small and esteemed within the flock of the Messiah than, while seeming preeminent, to be cast out from his hope.

For thus says all-virtuous Wisdom: “Behold, I will bring forth to you a word from my breath, and I will teach you my message. Because I called and you did not listen, and I stretched out words and you paid no attention, but made my counsels void, and disobeyed my reproofs, therefore I also will laugh at your ruin; I will rejoice when destruction comes upon you, and when sudden turmoil reaches you, and your overthrow comes like a storm, or when affliction and siege come upon you. For it shall be that when you call upon me, I will not listen to you; evil men will seek me and will not find me. For they hated wisdom, and did not choose the fear of the Lord, nor were they willing to attend to my counsels, but they mocked my reproofs. Therefore they will eat the fruits of their own way and be filled with their own ungodliness. For because they were wronging the innocent, they will be slain, and examination will destroy the ungodly; but the one who hears me will dwell, trusting in hope, and will be at rest without fear from every evil.”

footnotes.text: Or 'to be found small and notable/recognized within the flock of the Messiah.' The Greek term can denote being accounted or held in regard.
'To be cast out from his hope' may also be understood as 'excluded from the hope in him.'
This extended citation echoes Proverbs 1:23-33 in a form close to the Greek wisdom tradition.
The Greek word rendered 'examination' is rare and difficult; it may also suggest 'scrutiny' or 'testing.'

chapters.text:
Let us therefore submit ourselves to his all-holy and glorious name, fleeing the threats previously spoken through wisdom against the disobedient, so that we may dwell secure, having placed our confidence in the most holy name of his majesty.

Accept our counsel, and you will have no cause for regret. For as God lives, and the Lord Jesus Messiah lives, and the Holy Spirit, who are the faith and the hope of the elect, so surely the one who in humility, with earnest gentleness, without regret carries out the righteous requirements and commandments given by God, this one will be enrolled and counted among the number of those being saved through Jesus Messiah, through whom is the glory to him forever and ever. Amen.

footnotes.text: Or 'through Wisdom,' if the phrase is taken as a more personified allusion rather than a general reference to wisdom as the medium of warning.
The clause 'who are the faith and the hope of the elect' is syntactically compressed in Greek and may also be understood appositionally: 'and the faith and hope of the elect.'
'Those being saved' renders a present participle, preserving the sense of an ongoing state rather than only a completed result.

chapters.text:
But if some disobey the things spoken by him through us, let them know that they will entangle themselves in transgression and in no small danger. But we will be innocent of this sin, and we will ask, making earnest prayer and supplication, that the Creator of all may keep intact the number of his elect that has been counted throughout the whole world, through his beloved child Jesus the Messiah, through whom he called us from darkness into light, from ignorance into the full knowledge of the glory of his name,

to hope in your name, the source from which all creation first came, having opened the eyes of our heart so that we might know you, the only Highest among the highest, holy, resting among the holy; the one who humbles the insolence of the proud, who breaks up the designs of the nations, who raises the lowly to high places and humbles the lofty, who makes rich and makes poor, who kills and gives life and causes to live, the only benefactor of spirits and God of all flesh; who looks into the depths, the overseer of human deeds, the helper of those in danger, the savior of those without hope, the creator and overseer of every spirit; who multiplies nations upon the earth and from among them all chooses those who love you through Jesus the Messiah, your beloved child, through whom you trained us, sanctified us, and honored us.

We ask you, Master, be our helper and defender. Rescue those of us who are in affliction, have mercy on the lowly, raise up the fallen, show yourself to those in need, heal the weak, turn back the wandering of your people. Feed the hungry, ransom our prisoners, raise up the sick, encourage the fainthearted. Let all the nations know that you alone are God, and that Jesus the Messiah is your child, and that we are your people and the sheep of your pasture.

footnotes.text: Or 'will bind themselves' or 'will involve themselves'; the Greek verb suggests bringing oneself into a state of liability.
'Beloved child' translates Greek παῖς, which can also mean 'servant' or, in some contexts, 'son.'
'Jesus the Messiah' could also be rendered 'Jesus Christ.'
The phrase rendered 'the source from which all creation first came' is difficult and may also be understood as 'the origin of all creation.'
Echoes biblical prayer language such as 'the sheep of your pasture' (cf. Ps 79:13; 95:7 LXX/MT numbering differs).

chapters.text: For you have made manifest the everlasting order of the world through your workings. You, Lord, created the inhabited earth, faithful throughout all generations, righteous in your judgments, marvelous in might and majesty, wise in creating and understanding in establishing the things that have come to be, good in the things that are seen and faithful toward those who have put their trust in you, merciful and compassionate. Forgive us our lawless deeds and our unrighteous acts and our trespasses and failings. 2 Do not count every sin of your male slaves and female slaves against them, but cleanse us with the cleansing of your truth, and direct our steps to walk in holiness of heart and to do the things that are good and pleasing before you and before our rulers. 3 Yes, Master, make your face shine upon us for good in peace, so that we may be sheltered by your mighty hand and delivered from every sin by your uplifted arm; and rescue us from those who hate us unjustly. 4 Give harmony and peace to us and to all who dwell on the earth, just as you gave them to our fathers when they called upon you in holiness, in faith and truth, becoming obedient to your almighty and all-virtuous name, and to our rulers and leaders upon the earth.

footnotes.text: The Greek literally says 'your male slaves and female slaves,' a gendered pair for God's dependent people.
'Make your face shine upon us' echoes biblical blessing language; compare Num 6:25; Ps 67:1(66:2 LXX).
The phrase 'all-virtuous name' renders a rare Greek compound emphasizing the complete excellence of God's name.

chapters.text: You, Master, gave them the authority of rule through your magnificent and indescribable might, so that, knowing the glory and honor given to them by you, we might submit ourselves to them, opposing your will in nothing. Grant to them, Lord, health, peace, concord, and stability, so that they may administer without offense the leadership you have given them. For you, Master, heavenly King of the ages, give glory and honor and authority over the things on earth to the sons of men. You, Lord, direct their counsel according to what is good and pleasing before you, so that, administering in peace and gentleness, in a godly way, the authority you have given them, they may obtain your favor. You alone are able to do these things and greater good things for us; to you we give thanks through the high priest and guardian of our souls, Jesus the Messiah, through whom be the glory and the majesty to you both now and from generation to generation and unto the ages of ages. Amen.

footnotes.text: Or 'royal authority' for 'the authority of rule.'
'Sons of men' preserves the scriptural cadence of the Greek phrase and refers to human beings generally.
Or 'they may obtain your mercy' at 'they may obtain your favor.'
Compare 1 Pet 2:13–17 and 1 Tim 2:1–2 for early Christian prayer and submission in relation to rulers.

chapters.text: Now concerning the matters that pertain to our religion, and that are most beneficial for a virtuous life to those who desire to order their way in piety and righteousness, we have written to you sufficiently, brothers. For concerning faith and repentance and genuine love and self-control and sound-mindedness and endurance, we have touched on every point, reminding you that you ought to please the Almighty God in holiness, in righteousness and truth and longsuffering, being of one mind, free from resentment, in love and peace with earnest gentleness, just as also our fathers, who were spoken of before, were well-pleasing to him, humbling themselves in what concerns the Father and God and Creator and all people. And we have urged these things all the more because we clearly knew that we were writing to faithful and highly regarded men who have been deeply instructed in the sayings of God's training.

footnotes.text: Or 'our worship' or 'our devotion' for 'our religion.'
The address 'brothers' renders a masculine Greek form; the chapter is addressed to the Corinthian church as a body.
'Free from resentment' could also be rendered 'not bearing grudges' or 'without remembering wrongs.'
'The sayings of God's training' may also be rendered 'the oracles of God's instruction.'

chapters.text: Therefore it is fitting that, having come near to such and so many examples, we should bow the neck and, taking our place under obedience, should reach the goal set before us in truth, without any blemish. 2. For you will give us joy and exultation if, becoming obedient to the things written by us through the Holy Spirit, you cut off the lawless anger of your jealousy, in accordance with the appeal that we made in this letter concerning peace and concord. 3. And we have sent faithful and prudent men, who from youth until old age have conducted themselves blamelessly among us; these will also be witnesses between you and us. 4. And we have done this so that you may know that all our care has been, and is, for this: that you may quickly be restored to peace.

footnotes.text: Or 'envy' for 'jealousy'; the Greek term can denote either, but here it is clearly negative in context.
'Through the Holy Spirit' expresses Clement's strong claim that the letter's admonition is Spirit-mediated, not merely personal advice.
The final clause may also be rendered 'that you may quickly make peace' or 'that you may quickly be at peace.'

chapters.text: Finally, may the all-seeing God and Master of spirits and Lord of all flesh, who chose the Lord Jesus Messiah and us through him to be a people for his own possession, grant to every soul that has been called by his magnificent and holy name faith, fear, peace, endurance and longsuffering, self-control, purity, and sound judgment, so as to be pleasing to his name through our high priest and protector Jesus Messiah, through whom be glory and majesty to him, might and honor, both now and through all the ages of ages. Amen.

footnotes.text: Or 'every soul that calls upon his magnificent and holy name.' The Greek participle may be taken in relation to invoking the name, though the passive sense 'called by' better fits the form.
'A people for his own possession' echoes covenant language from the Greek Scriptures; compare expressions in Exodus 19:5 and Deuteronomy 7:6.
The final doxology most likely gives glory to God through Jesus Messiah, though some may hear the wording as more directly christological because of the immediately preceding relative clause.

chapters.text: Now send back to us quickly, in peace and with joy, the men sent from us—Claudius Ephebus and Valerius Bito, together with Fortunatus—so that they may report to us all the sooner the peace and concord for which we have prayed earnestly.

footnotes.text: The Greek text provided for this chapter breaks off after 'and' (καὶ). The closing phrase in English is a conservative completion of the evident sense of the purpose clause.
'Peace and concord' reflects the reconciliation theme of the letter and likely stands behind the truncated ending here.
